proposed_hypotheses.text: The overall objective_response rate in the cohort is below 50% (i.e., the outcome is the minority class), so analyses should treat objective_response as a binary outcome with logistic models.

analyses.code: df['objective_response'].value_counts()
analyses.result_summary: objective_response is binary 0/1 with 8447 responders and 41553 non-responders out of 50000 (overall response rate 16.89%). The cohort has 125 columns: 1 patient_id, 4 categorical (object), 35 continuous (float), 7 ordinal/count int, 77 binary int, plus the outcome.

proposed_hypotheses.text: At least one of the 77 binary features is associated with objective_response at a Bonferroni-corrected level (p < 0.05/77).
feature_013 (a high-prevalence binary feature, ~65% of cohort) is associated with LOWER odds of objective_response.

analyses.code: for c in binary_feats: sm.Logit(y, sm.add_constant(df[c])).fit()
analyses.result_summary: Univariate logistic screen of 77 binary features. 8 features reach nominal p<0.05; 4 survive Bonferroni (p<6.5e-4): feature_013 (OR=0.744, p=1.3e-33), feature_067 (OR=0.777, p=2.0e-18), feature_006 (OR=1.136, p=9.8e-7), feature_007 (OR=1.086, p=5.7e-4). feature_013 RR=19.6% when 0 vs 15.4% when 1.

proposed_hypotheses.text: At least one of the 35 continuous features is associated with objective_response at a Bonferroni-corrected level (p < 0.05/35).
feature_011 (right-skewed continuous, range 0–28) is associated with LOWER odds of objective_response — patients with higher feature_011 respond less often.

analyses.code: for c in cont_feats: sm.Logit(y, sm.add_constant(df[c])).fit()
analyses.result_summary: Univariate logistic screen of 35 continuous features. 4 reach nominal p<0.05; 3 survive Bonferroni (p<1.4e-3): feature_011 (coef=-0.039 per unit, p=1.8e-32; mean 3.93 in non-responders vs 3.38 in responders), feature_099 (coef=+0.091, p=1.4e-4), feature_063 (coef=-0.0049, p=6.9e-4). feature_092 nominally significant (coef=+0.166, p=3.2e-3).

proposed_hypotheses.text: feature_051 (3-level ordinal, values 0/1/2 with proportions 35/50/15) is associated with LOWER objective_response odds at higher levels — i.e., higher feature_051 score predicts worse response, consistent with a severity/performance-status-like measure.

analyses.code: sm.Logit(y, sm.add_constant(df['feature_051'])).fit()
analyses.result_summary: Treating feature_051 as numeric ordinal: coef=-0.375 per level, p=1.7e-93 — by far the strongest single predictor. Response rates are 21.1% at level 0, 15.7% at level 1, 11.1% at level 2 (monotonic decrease).

proposed_hypotheses.text: Among the categorical features (feature_057 smoking-like, feature_043 histology-like, feature_123 race-like, feature_005 insurance-like), at least one is associated with objective_response.

analyses.code: for c in obj_feats: stats.chi2_contingency(pd.crosstab(df[c], y))
analyses.result_summary: Chi-square tests: feature_057 (current/former/never smoker, 3 levels) p=0.27; feature_043 (adenocarcinoma vs squamous) p=0.078; feature_123 (race, 5 levels) p=0.045 (marginal); feature_005 (insurance, 4 levels) p=0.23. Only feature_123 reaches nominal significance, and the response-rate spread is small (16.5% white to 18.5% other).

proposed_hypotheses.text: When the strongest univariate predictors (feature_013, feature_067, feature_006, feature_007, feature_051, feature_011, feature_099, feature_063, feature_092) are entered into a single multivariable logistic regression, each retains an independent association with objective_response in the same direction as its univariate effect — i.e., effects are largely additive rather than confounded.

analyses.code: smf.logit('objective_response ~ feature_013 + feature_067 + feature_006 + feature_007 + feature_051 + f011_z + f099_z + f063_z + f092_z + ...', df).fit()
analyses.result_summary: Multivariable logistic model with the top predictors (pseudo-R^2 = 0.020, LLR p=4.6e-186). Each of feature_013 (coef=-0.302, p<1e-33), feature_067 (-0.259, p<1e-18), feature_006 (+0.130, p=8e-7), feature_007 (+0.080, p=9e-4), feature_051 (-0.382, p<1e-94), feature_011 (z) (-0.153, p<1e-32), feature_099 (z) (+0.046, p=1e-4), feature_063 (z) (-0.046, p=5e-4), and feature_092 (z) (+0.037, p=2e-3) remains significant with the same sign as its univariate estimate. Adjustment barely shifts coefficients, so the predictors carry largely non-overlapping information.

proposed_hypotheses.text: The negative effect of feature_013 on objective_response is HETEROGENEOUS across feature_051 strata — specifically, the OR for feature_013 is more strongly negative at low feature_051 (level 0) than at high feature_051 (level 2), implying a feature_013 × feature_051 interaction.

analyses.code: smf.logit('objective_response ~ feature_013 * C(feature_051)', df).fit() vs nested
analyses.result_summary: Stratified ORs for feature_013: level 0 OR=0.754 (p=1e-13), level 1 OR=0.710 (p=4e-22), level 2 OR=0.837 (p=0.019). The effect is somewhat attenuated at level 2, but the LRT for interaction is non-significant: chi2=4.31, df=2, p=0.116. Hypothesis h9 is NOT supported — feature_013's effect is essentially uniform across feature_051 strata.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Response rate decreases monotonically with feature_011 across binned values of feature_011 (i.e., a clean dose–response), confirming that the linear logistic effect is not driven by an outlier bin.

analyses.code: df.groupby(pd.cut(df['feature_011'], bins=[-.1,.1,1,3,5,10,30]))['objective_response'].mean()
analyses.result_summary: Response rate by feature_011 bin: =0 → 19.3%; (0,1] → 18.3%; (1,3] → 17.0%; (3,5] → 17.0%; (5,10] → 15.1%; >10 → 12.8%. Monotone-decreasing dose-response from ~19% to ~13% across the range, confirming a real gradient (Cochran-Armitage-like trend p < 1e-20 implied by the linear logistic fit, p=1.8e-32).

proposed_hypotheses.text: feature_006 and feature_007 (both binary, both univariately positive) have a SUPER-ADDITIVE (positive) interaction on objective_response — patients with both features set respond at a rate higher than additive prediction.

analyses.code: smf.logit('objective_response ~ feature_006 * feature_007', df).fit() vs nested
analyses.result_summary: Joint response rates: (006=0,007=0) 16.1%, (0,1) 16.7%, (1,0) 16.9%, (1,1) 19.7%. Interaction coefficient = +0.156, LRT chi2=8.15, p=0.0043. The (1,1) cell exceeds additive prediction, supporting a synergistic interaction. The interaction term remains significant in the full multivariable model (coef=+0.156, p=0.003).

proposed_hypotheses.text: feature_013 (the strongest binary predictor) interacts with at least one of the other 76 binary features in modifying objective_response, beyond what is expected by chance under a 5% FWER.

analyses.code: for c in binary_feats: LRT(logit('y ~ feature_013*c') vs logit('y ~ feature_013 + c'))
analyses.result_summary: Screen of feature_013 × every other binary feature (76 tests). Only 2 tests reach nominal p<0.05 (feature_034 p=0.008, feature_120 p=0.032), which is BELOW the chance expectation of ~3.8 false positives. None survives multiple-testing correction. Hypothesis h12 is NOT supported — feature_013's effect is essentially homogeneous across binary subgroups.

proposed_hypotheses.text: feature_051 (the strongest ordinal predictor) interacts with at least one of the 76 binary features in modifying objective_response, beyond chance expectation.

analyses.code: for c in binary_feats: LRT(logit('y ~ feature_051*c') vs logit('y ~ feature_051 + c'))
analyses.result_summary: Screen of feature_051 × every other binary feature (76 tests). Only 1 test reaches nominal p<0.05 (feature_102 p=0.030), which is well below the ~3.8 expected by chance. None survives multiple-testing correction. Hypothesis h13 is NOT supported — feature_051's effect is essentially homogeneous across binary subgroups.

proposed_hypotheses.text: The continuous predictor feature_011 has a heterogeneous effect across binary subgroups — at least one feature_011 × binary_feature interaction is significant beyond chance.

analyses.code: for c in binary_feats: LRT(logit('y ~ f011_z*c') vs logit('y ~ f011_z + c'))
analyses.result_summary: Screen of feature_011 × every binary feature (77 tests). Only 1 nominally significant interaction (feature_049, p=0.020), well below the ~3.85 expected by chance. The negative gradient in feature_011 is uniform across the cohort.

proposed_hypotheses.text: The effects of feature_013, feature_067, feature_006, feature_051, and feature_011 on objective_response are CONSISTENT across feature_043 (histology: adenocarcinoma vs squamous) — i.e., no major histology heterogeneity.

analyses.code: for h in df['feature_043'].unique(): for f in top_predictors: smf.logit(...).fit(); also LRT for interaction with histology.
analyses.result_summary: In adenocarcinoma (n=36099): feature_013 coef=-0.308, feature_067=-0.244, feature_006=+0.133, feature_051=-0.361, feature_011 (z)=-0.146; in squamous (n=13901): feature_013=-0.263, feature_067=-0.272, feature_006=+0.105, feature_051=-0.410, feature_011 (z)=-0.156. All effects same direction and similar magnitude. LRT for feature_013 × histology: chi2=0.70, p=0.40. Hypothesis h15 SUPPORTED.

proposed_hypotheses.text: The effect of feature_013 on objective_response differs across feature_123 (race) groups — i.e., the OR is not constant across white / black / hispanic / asian / other.

analyses.code: for r in df['feature_123'].unique(): smf.logit('y ~ feature_013', df[df.feature_123==r]).fit()
analyses.result_summary: feature_013 OR by race: white 0.731 (p=1e-24, n=32607), black 0.730 (p=5e-6, n=6008), hispanic 0.781 (p=9e-5, n=7392), asian 0.854 (p=0.11, n=2979), other 0.689 (p=0.025, n=1014). All point estimates lie in 0.69–0.85 — consistent direction. The asian-only OR is closest to 1, but the confidence intervals overlap heavily. Race is not a major effect modifier.

proposed_hypotheses.text: The negative effect of feature_011 on objective_response is consistent across feature_057 (smoking status) strata — current, former, and never smokers all show similar slopes.

analyses.code: for s in df['feature_057'].unique(): smf.logit('y ~ f011_z', df[df.feature_057==s]).fit(); LRT for f011_z*feature_057.
analyses.result_summary: feature_011 (z) coef by smoking: former -0.164 (p=4e-22), current -0.125 (p=4e-8), never -0.143 (p=1e-5). All negative and similar. LRT for interaction f011_z × feature_057: chi2=1.93, df=2, p=0.38. Hypothesis h17 SUPPORTED — feature_011 effect homogeneous across smoking groups.

proposed_hypotheses.text: feature_063 is right-skewed (median 3.3, max 297) and a log-transform yields a stronger and more linear association with the outcome than the raw scale.

analyses.code: sm.Logit(y, sm.add_constant(np.log1p(df['feature_063']))).fit()
analyses.result_summary: log(feature_063+1) coef = -0.071 per log-unit, p=1.8e-6. Z-score in the multivariable model: coef=-0.060 per SD of log, p=4.9e-7. The log scale yields a more significant association than the raw scale (p=6.9e-4) and is a better functional form for this skewed measure.

proposed_hypotheses.text: feature_099 (continuous, narrow range 1.7–5.5, mean 3.8) is POSITIVELY associated with objective_response — higher feature_099 → higher odds of response.

analyses.code: smf.logit('objective_response ~ feature_099', df).fit()
analyses.result_summary: feature_099 coef=+0.091 per unit (p=1.4e-4); per SD coef=+0.046 (p=1.5e-4). Mean is 3.798 in non-responders vs 3.821 in responders — small absolute difference but significant given n=50000. Hypothesis h19 SUPPORTED.

proposed_hypotheses.text: feature_092 (continuous, range 0–0.8, looks like a proportion) has a NON-LINEAR association with objective_response: response is highest at the high tail (>0.5), suggesting a threshold-like enrichment biomarker rather than a strictly linear gradient.

analyses.code: df.groupby(pd.cut(df.feature_092, bins=[-.001,.01,.1,.5,1.0]))['objective_response'].mean()
analyses.result_summary: Binned response rates by feature_092: <=0.01 → 17.06%; 0.01–0.10 → 16.76%; 0.10–0.50 → 16.38%; >0.50 → 18.23%. Pattern is U-shaped/J-shaped, not strictly monotone — the >0.5 bin is the highest. Linear logistic coef +0.166 per unit (p=3.2e-3) captures the upward tilt at the top end. The plateau in the 0.10–0.50 range hints at a thresholded enrichment biomarker.

proposed_hypotheses.text: feature_078 (continuous, mean 65, std 10, range 30–90 — likely age in years) is associated with objective_response.

analyses.code: smf.logit('objective_response ~ feature_078', df).fit()
analyses.result_summary: feature_078 coef=+0.00051 per unit, p=0.67. Response rates by age bin: <=50 16.6%, 50–60 16.9%, 60–70 17.0%, 70–80 16.5%, 80+ 18.2% — essentially flat with a small uptick in the oldest decile. Hypothesis h21 NOT supported: feature_078 is unrelated to objective_response in this cohort.

proposed_hypotheses.text: feature_064 (integer 0–10, looks like a count, e.g., number of metastatic sites or number of prior medications) is NEGATIVELY associated with objective_response — patients with higher counts respond less.

analyses.code: df.groupby('feature_064')['objective_response'].mean(); sm.Logit(y, sm.add_constant(df['feature_064'])).fit()
analyses.result_summary: feature_064 coef=+0.0013 per unit, p=0.78 — null. Response rate is essentially flat from level 0 (16.98%) through 10 (18.08%) with no monotonic trend. Hypothesis h22 NOT supported. Whatever feature_064 measures, it does not affect response in this cohort.

proposed_hypotheses.text: feature_067 (binary, 25% prevalence, OR=0.78 univariately) is the second-strongest binary negative predictor and remains independent of feature_013 — i.e., the two effects are additive on the log-odds scale.

analyses.code: smf.logit('objective_response ~ feature_013 + feature_067', df).fit() vs interaction
analyses.result_summary: In the additive bivariate model: feature_013 coef=-0.293 (p<1e-32), feature_067 coef=-0.246 (p<1e-15) — both retain near-univariate magnitude, indicating they are largely independent. LRT for feature_013 × feature_067 interaction: p=0.36 (not significant). Hypothesis h23 SUPPORTED — additive log-odds.

proposed_hypotheses.text: After adjusting for the strong predictors (feature_013, feature_067, feature_006, feature_007, feature_051, feature_011, feature_063, feature_099, feature_092) and demographics (race, smoking, histology), feature_123 (race) and feature_057 (smoking) are NOT independently associated with objective_response.

analyses.code: smf.logit('objective_response ~ ... + C(feature_123) + smk_current + smk_never + hist + ...', df).fit()
analyses.result_summary: In the fully adjusted model: feature_123 race coefficients all p>0.13 (black 0.93, hispanic 0.49, other 0.62, white 0.13); smk_current p=0.65; smk_never p=0.97; hist (squamous) p=0.16. None of the demographic covariates is independently significant once the biomarker/clinical predictors are in the model. The marginal univariate p=0.045 for race is fully accounted for by the other variables. Hypothesis h24 SUPPORTED.

proposed_hypotheses.text: feature_039 (binary, 50% prevalence, OR ≈ 1.07 univariately) retains an independent positive association with objective_response in the multivariable model — i.e., it is not a confounded pass-through of one of the stronger binary predictors.

analyses.code: smf.logit('objective_response ~ feature_039 + ...adjusters...', df).fit()
analyses.result_summary: Fully adjusted feature_039 coef=+0.076, p=0.002 (vs univariate +0.070, p=0.003). The adjusted estimate is essentially unchanged, so feature_039 carries an independent positive signal not absorbed by feature_013 / feature_067 / feature_051 / feature_011. Hypothesis h25 SUPPORTED.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Two more binary features identified in the univariate screen — feature_076 (positive, low prevalence ~8%) and feature_112 (negative, low prevalence ~8%) — retain modest independent effects in the adjusted multivariable model.

analyses.code: (coefficients for feature_076 and feature_112 from the final adjusted multivariable model)
analyses.result_summary: In the final adjusted model: feature_076 coef=+0.099, p=0.022 (univariate +0.100, p=0.018) — positive small effect retained; feature_112 coef=-0.102, p=0.027 (univariate -0.099, p=0.031) — negative small effect retained. Both consistent with their univariate signals. Hypothesis h26 SUPPORTED — these low-prevalence markers carry independent (if modest) information.

proposed_hypotheses.text: A parsimonious final model containing feature_013, feature_067, feature_006, feature_007 (with a 006×007 interaction), feature_039, feature_076, feature_112, feature_051, feature_011, feature_099, log(feature_063+1), and feature_092, plus race/smoking/histology covariates, captures essentially all of the predictable variation in objective_response in this cohort, with pseudo-R² ≈ 0.02 and no further single covariate adding more than trivial signal.

analyses.code: smf.logit('objective_response ~ feature_013 + feature_067 + feature_006*feature_007 + feature_039 + feature_076 + feature_112 + feature_021 + feature_051 + f011_z + f099_z + f063_logz + f092_z + hist + smk_current + smk_never + C(feature_123)', df).fit()
analyses.result_summary: Final adjusted multivariable logistic model (n=50000, 21 df, pseudo-R²=0.0207, LLR p=1.6e-185). Significant adjusted predictors: feature_051 (-0.381, p<1e-94), feature_013 (-0.302, p<1e-33), feature_067 (-0.260, p<1e-18), feature_011-z (-0.153, p<1e-32), feature_006:feature_007 interaction (+0.156, p=0.003), feature_063-log-z (-0.060, p=9e-7), feature_099-z (+0.046, p=1e-4), feature_039 (+0.076, p=0.002), feature_092-z (+0.037, p=0.002), feature_076 (+0.099, p=0.022), feature_112 (-0.102, p=0.027). Demographics (race, smoking, histology) are all NS once biomarkers/clinical predictors are included. The total cohort-level pseudo-R² of 0.02 indicates the predictable signal is real (LLR p < 1e-180) but small in absolute terms — most of the variance in objective_response is unexplained by these features.
